title: Fear of Nature: an Emerging Threat to Conservation
subtitle: 当孩子害怕蝴蝶：代际遗忘如何侵蚀自然保护

paragraphs.en: What do we lose when natural spaces and species disappear? Increasingly, research has shown that as species and ecosystems vanish, it also chips away at our ability to preserve what remains — because we no longer understand what we're losing.
paragraphs.zh: 当自然空间和物种消失时，我们究竟失去了什么？越来越多的研究表明，随着物种和生态系统的消亡，我们保护剩余自然的能力也在被逐渐削弱——因为我们已不再理解自己正在失去的东西。

paragraphs.en: You probably see it all the time. The neighbor who puts pesticides on his lawn rather than deal with annoying bees. The politician who votes against wildlife protection because she's never seen a wolf in the wild. The corporation that wants to bulldoze the habitat of a rare frog.
paragraphs.zh: 这种现象或许随处可见。邻居宁愿在草坪上喷洒杀虫剂，也不愿应付恼人的蜜蜂；政客因从未在野外见过狼而投票反对野生动物保护；企业则想用推土机铲平稀有青蛙的栖息地。

paragraphs.en: At best this can be termed "the extinction of experience," where our cultural and natural histories fade from our memories and therefore our reality. At its worst it becomes something even more concerning: "biophobia," the fear of living things and a complete aversion to nature.
paragraphs.zh: 往好里说，这可以被称为“体验的灭绝”——我们的文化和自然历史从记忆中消退，进而从现实中消失。往坏里说，它则会演变为更令人担忧的“生物恐惧症”，即对生物的恐惧和对自然的全然厌恶。

paragraphs.en: This isn't the fiction of living in a cold, empty dystopia. Sadly, it's becoming a way of life for too many people — especially children. A recent study in Japan paints a striking portrait of this problem. A survey of more than 5,300 school children in the Tochigi Prefecture examined their perception of 14 local insect species and one spider.
paragraphs.zh: 这并非什么生活在冰冷空虚的反乌托邦世界中的虚构故事。可悲的是，它正成为太多人——尤其是儿童——的一种生活方式。日本近期一项研究就为此描绘了一幅触目惊心的图景。该研究对栃木县5300多名学童进行了调查，了解他们对14种本地昆虫和一种蜘蛛的看法。

words.meaning: n. 反乌托邦；绝望的社会

paragraphs.en: The results? A collective "ew!" Most of the students saw the species as things to dislike or fear, or even as sources of danger. The less experience the students had with nature, the more negative their feelings. The results were published earlier this year in the journal Biological Conservation.
paragraphs.zh: 结果如何？孩子们的反应是一片“咦——恶心！”大多数学生将这些物种视为讨厌或害怕的对象，甚至视作危险的来源。学生接触自然的经历越少，其负面情绪就越强烈。该研究结果于今年早些时候发表在《生物保护》期刊上。

paragraphs.en: Lead researcher Masashi Soga with the University of Tokyo says the study stemmed from observations about today's nature-deficient children. "Humans inherently avoid dangerous organisms such as bees, but children these days avoid even harmless animals such as butterflies and dragonflies." he says. "I have long wondered why so many of today's children react like this."
paragraphs.zh: 该研究首席作者、东京大学的曾我昌史表示，这项研究源于对当今儿童普遍缺乏自然接触的观察。他说：“人类天生会避开蜜蜂等危险生物，但如今的孩子甚至会避开蝴蝶和蜻蜓这类无害动物。我长久以来一直想知道，为什么今天会有这么多孩子产生这样的反应。”

paragraphs.en: Although the children's reactions were somewhat expected, the new study did contain an unexpected finding: Many of the surveyed children revealed that their parents also expressed fear or disgust of the same animals. In fact these parental emotions were strong enough to overwhelm any positive experiences the children might have gained from direct experiences in nature.
paragraphs.zh: 尽管孩子们的反应在某种程度上在意料之中，但新研究仍有一个意外发现：许多受访孩子透露，他们的父母同样对这些动物表示恐惧或厌恶。事实上，父母的这些情绪强烈到足以压倒孩子本可以从直接接触自然中获得的任何积极体验。

paragraphs.en: As Soga and his coauthors wrote in their paper, "Our results suggest that there is likely a feedback loop in which an increase in people who have negative attitudes towards nature in one generation will lead to a further increase in people with similar attitudes in the next generation." And that's possibly the greater threat posed by extinction of experience.
paragraphs.zh: 正如曾我与合著者在论文中所写：“我们的结果表明，可能存在一个反馈循环：一代人对自然持消极态度的人数增加，将导致下一代持相似态度的人数进一步增加。”而这或许才是体验灭绝所带来的更大威胁。

paragraphs.en: Soga suggests the generational loss — a condition previously dubbed environmental generational amnesia — could chip away at our societal ability to preserve what we're losing. "I believe that increased biophobia is a major, but invisible, threat to global biodiversity," Soga says. "As the number of children who have biophobia increases, public interest and support for biodiversity conservation will gradually decline."
paragraphs.zh: 曾我指出，这种代际损失——此前被称为“环境代际遗忘症”——可能会逐步削弱社会保护正在流失之物的能力。他说：“我认为日益加剧的生物恐惧症是对全球生物多样性的一个重大却又无形的威胁。随着患有生物恐惧症的儿童数量增加，公众对生物多样性保护的兴趣和支持都将逐渐下降。”

paragraphs.en: Although many conservation biologists still consider that preventing the loss of wildlife habitat is the most important way to conserve biodiversity, I think preventing increased biophobia is also important for conservation. What's to be done about this? The paper makes several recommendations, the most obvious of which is that children should experience nature more often.
paragraphs.zh: 尽管许多保护生物学家仍认为防止野生动物栖息地丧失是保护生物多样性最重要的途径，但我认为防止生物恐惧症加剧对保护工作同样重要。对此我们能做些什么？论文提出了若干建议，其中最显而易见的就是让孩子更频繁地接触自然。

paragraphs.en: The authors also suggest establishing policies to guide these natural experiences and increasing educational programs about the natural world. Helping parents to see species around them in a new light would make a difference, too. And, of course, maintaining support for preserving the wild spaces where these "scary" creatures live is the most important thing of all.
paragraphs.zh: 作者还建议制定相关政策来引导这些自然体验，并增加关于自然界的学习项目。帮助父母以新的眼光看待身边的物种，同样会产生积极影响。当然，最关键的还是要持续支持保护这些“可怕”生物所栖息的荒野空间。

paragraphs.en: That's a point reinforced by another recent study, which found that wild spaces located within urban areas — and the plants and animals that thrive in them — are particularly important for human health and well-being. Published in the journal Frontiers in Sustainable Cities, the study examined attitudes toward Discovery Park, the heavily forested 534-acre public park in Seattle, Washington.
paragraphs.zh: 另一项近期研究也强化了这一观点。该研究发现，城市区域内的荒野空间——以及在其中繁衍生息的动植物——对人类健康和福祉尤为重要。这项发表于《可持续城市前沿》期刊的研究考察了人们对华盛顿州西雅图市占地534英亩、森林茂密的探索公园的态度。

paragraphs.en: It found that the public had the most appreciation for — and gained the most value from — the wildest parts of the park. "I have seen whales, seals, fish, eagles, shorebirds and many other sea creatures in their natural habitat," one survey participant wrote. "Coming here with people has allowed me to connect and talk with them about conversation that simply does not happen in everyday life." wrote another.
paragraphs.zh: 研究发现，公众对公园中最荒芜原始的区域最为欣赏，从中获得的体验价值也最高。一位调查参与者写道：“我在它们的自然栖息地看到了鲸、海豹、鱼、鹰、滨鸟和许多其他海洋生物。”另一位则写道：“和别人一起来到这里，让我们有机会进行交流与对话，而这些对话在日常生活中根本不会发生。”

paragraphs.en: The participants reported that their most valuable experiences in the park included encountering wildlife, walking through open spaces, exploring the beach and finding beautiful views. "We saw that a large majority of participants' interactions, especially their most meaningful interactions, depended on Discovery Park's relative wildness," says lead author Elizabeth Lev, a master's student in the University of Washington's Human Interaction with Nature Lab.
paragraphs.zh: 参与者报告称，他们在公园里最有价值的体验包括邂逅野生动物、穿越开阔地带、探索海滩以及发现美景。该研究第一作者、华盛顿大学人类与自然互动实验室硕士生伊丽莎白·列夫表示：“我们发现，绝大多数参与者的互动体验，尤其是最有意义的那些，都有赖于探索公园相对原始的自然状态。”

paragraphs.en: This is only possible because the park is relatively wild. After all, you can't enjoy watching birds if there are no birds to follow; gaze at the sunset if it's obscured by skyscrapers; or stop and smell the flowers if they don't have room to grow. And yet even this long-protected space could someday become less hospitable to nature.
paragraphs.zh: 这一切之所以可能，正是因为公园保留了相对原始的自然风貌。毕竟，若无鸟可寻，便无从观鸟之乐；若日落被摩天大楼遮蔽，便无从凝视夕阳；若花无地生长，便无从驻足闻香。然而，即便是这片长久受到保护的空间，也可能终有一天变得不再适宜自然生息。

paragraphs.en: Over the past few years a lot of people and organizations have suggested developing parts of Discovery Park or the neighboring area. Most recently a plan proposed building 34 acres of much-needed affordable housing and parking spaces adjacent to the park, bringing with them noise, traffic and pollution. If anything like that happened, both the park and the people of Seattle could lose something vital.
paragraphs.zh: 过去几年里，许多个人和组织建议对探索公园或邻近区域进行部分开发。最近有一项计划提议在公园旁建造34英亩急需的经济适用房和停车场，这随之会带来噪音、交通和污染问题。倘若发生类似情况，公园和西雅图民众都可能失去一些至关重要的东西。

paragraphs.en: And that would continue the trend of chipping away at Seattle's — and the world's — natural spaces leaving just tiny pocket parks and green-but-empty spaces that offer little real value to wildlife, plants or people. "It is true that any interaction with nature is better than none, but I don't want people to be satisfied with any small bit of grass and trees," Lev says.
paragraphs.zh: 而这将继续侵蚀西雅图乃至全球自然空间的趋势，最终只剩下零星的口袋公园和徒有其绿的空旷地带，对野生动植物和人类都几乎不再具有真正的价值。列夫说：“诚然，与自然有任何形式的接触总比完全没有好，但我不希望人们满足于区区一小片草皮和几棵树。”

paragraphs.en: "We have been in this cycle of environmental generational amnesia for a long time, where the baseline keeps shifting and we don't even realize what we're losing until it's gone. If we can get people to understand how much meaning and value can come from having more experiences with more wild forms of nature, then maybe we can stop this cycle and move toward conserving and restoring what we have left."
paragraphs.zh: “我们早已陷入环境代际遗忘症的循环之中，参照的基线不断偏移，直到事物彻底消失，我们甚至都意识不到自己失去了什么。如果我们能让人们理解，与更原始的自然形态进行更多接触能带来多么深远的意义与价值，那么我们或许就能终止这一循环，转而致力于保护和恢复我们所剩无几的自然。”

paragraphs.en: Building this understanding in an ever-more fearful and disconnected world may be the biggest challenge. Peter Kahn, the senior author of Lev's paper and the director of the Human Interaction with Nature lab, made several suggestions for bridging this gap in this 2011 book, Technological Nature. They echo the recommendation about getting children into nature, but also include telling stories of how things used to be, imagining what things might be like in the future, and developing a common language about nature.
paragraphs.zh: 在一个日益恐惧且疏离自然的世界中建立这种认知，或许才是最大的挑战。列夫论文的资深作者、人类与自然互动实验室主任彼得·卡恩在其2011年出版的《技术自然》一书中，为弥合这一鸿沟提出了若干建议。这些建议与让孩子走进自然的呼吁相呼应，同时还包括讲述过去事物模样的故事、想象未来的可能面貌，以及发展一套关于自然的共同语言。

paragraphs.en: No matter what techniques we use, this growing field of research illustrates that saving nature requires encouraging people to experience it more often and more deeply. That calls for additional research — Lev and her coauthors have published a toolkit that other municipalities can follow to study the value of their own wild spaces — and clear communication of the results.
paragraphs.zh: 无论采取何种方法，这一日益发展的研究领域都表明，拯救自然需要鼓励人们更频繁、更深入地亲身体验它。这需要开展更多研究——列夫及其合著者已发布了一套工具包，可供其他城市参考以研究自身荒野空间的价值——同时也需要清晰有效地传达研究成果。

paragraphs.en: "If we can continue to show people the benefits of these wild spaces," Lev says, "maybe people will begin to see more value in keeping these areas undeveloped — for the sake of our mutual benefit."
paragraphs.zh: 列夫说：“如果我们能持续向人们展示这些荒野空间的益处，也许为了我们共同的利益，人们会开始意识到保持这些区域原始未开发状态具有更大的价值。”
